video_title: Charles Hoskinson: AI Agents Will Run Crypto
gist: Cardano founder Charles Hoskinson argues mainstream crypto adoption will come not from migrating users to new layer-1s but from AI agents transacting on users' behalf across abstracted chains, with privacy (zero-knowledge) as the control layer. He positions Cardano's Midnight as the cross-chain coordinator/middleman that earns fees facilitating intents-based settlement, Bitcoin DeFi, and compliant 'Web 2.5' enterprise flows.

themes.theme: AI agents as the crypto adoption vector

insights.claim: Hoskinson argues that long-term, mainstream users will only interact with cryptocurrencies through AI agents that hold delegated authority, not through wallets and seed phrases directly, making agent infrastructure the real adoption driver.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Bet on agent-payment rails and abstraction layers rather than on getting retail users to learn self-custody; agent-native protocols are the adoption play.
insights.quote: The only way long-term people are going to use cryptocurrencies is through agents. They're going to delegate authority to AI to do this on their behalf.

insights.claim: Agents are a near-perfect complement to crypto because agents are non-deterministic and creative while blockchains are deterministic and rule-based, and combining them yields a neuro-symbolic system with both provable rules and creative ambiguity-handling.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Frames the agent-plus-blockchain stack as the defensible architecture; watch projects fusing LLM agents with on-chain proof systems.
insights.quote: Blockchains are deterministic, they're rule-based systems... So, if you could put these two things together, you basically create a perfect neuro-symbolic AI system where you get the best of both worlds.

insights.claim: Agents reduce crypto consumer risk because an AI never tires, distinguishes a zero from an 'O' in an address, resists spoofed websites, and cannot be socially engineered when paired with proof systems, so consumer protection actually increases under agent delegation.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Security/UX is the wedge for agent adoption; protocols that harden agents against phishing and address errors gain a safety narrative regulators like.
insights.quote: The AI never gets tired... It knows the difference between an a zero and an O in terms of an address. It doesn't get confused by spoofed websites. You can't social engineer it if you have proof systems.

insights.claim: Hoskinson says Cardano/Midnight will support the OWS open agent standard in June/July and the X402 agent-payment standard, giving each agent its own wallet and the ability to spend autonomously.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Concrete near-term catalyst/timeline for ADA/Midnight; X402 and OWS are the agent-commerce standards to track for adoption across chains.
insights.quote: There's already open standards like OWS for this, and we're just going to support it as of June July here in Cardano Midnight... For example, the X402 standard.

insights.claim: Agent security is a 'public attack surface' problem identical to smart-contract security, so the same techniques (Docker/HSM sandboxing against prompt injection, public-code hardening) transfer one-to-one between the two domains.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Crypto-security teams have transferable edge in agent safety; prompt-injection containment is a fundable problem at the agent/chain intersection.
insights.quote: Agents have to be built for a public attack surface. So, the exact same security problems we have to resolve and the techniques we use are one-to-one applicable in the agentics world.

insights.claim: Hoskinson identifies the core agent paradox: agents must know you to be effective, but the more they know the more dangerous they are, which he says only privacy-preserving (zero-knowledge) sharing resolves for enterprise-scale agent use.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Enterprises with fiduciary duty over IP and customer data are the natural buyers of privacy-controlled agent frameworks; a B2B TAM thesis for ZK plus agents.
insights.quote: The paradox of agents is that to make them effective, they have to know you. But, the more they know about you, the more dangerous they are to you.

insights.claim: Midnight's privacy stack converges trusted execution environments (agent sandboxes), zero-knowledge proofs (proving behavior matches intent), and multi-party computation (letting agents coordinate without revealing state) as the toolkit for safe agent coordination.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Defines the specific cryptographic primitives underpinning the Midnight pitch; TEE/MPC/ZK convergence is the technical moat to evaluate.
insights.quote: We have to put trusted execution environments, multi-party computation, zero-knowledge proofs together in just the right flavor that you can build an infinitely scalable privacy system.

insights.claim: Hoskinson built 'Midnight City,' an agentic civilization where users deploy agents that autonomously form a society (marrying, divorcing, making child-support payments), used internally to stress-test X402, OWS and delegated-authority standards while serving as a consumer-facing front end for the privacy network.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: A novel go-to-market/testbed for agent standards plus a Tamagotchi-style consumer hook; watch engagement metrics ('watchability', 'empathy') as adoption proxies.
insights.quote: Literally, you can get an agent, you can put it in or bring your own agent, and then they just start running around doing things inside Midnight City.

themes.theme: Chain abstraction and the middleman thesis

insights.claim: Hoskinson argues the biggest fallacy in crypto is launching a new layer-1 expecting users to migrate; with 11 million tokens already issued, 550 million existing users will not move chains, so abstraction (not migration) is the only adoption path.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Bearish on new L1 token launches as adoption plays; bullish on abstraction/coordination infrastructure that captures users where they already are.
insights.quote: Everybody's launching a new layer one. There's 11 million tokens... 550 million users aren't going to magically migrate cuz you want them. That's the land of wishful thinking.

insights.claim: Hoskinson positions the largest value capture in crypto as controlling abstraction and cross-chain transactions (the 'middleman'), not running settlement, citing IBM ceding the OS to Microsoft as the analogy for why the connecting layer beats the underlying chain.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Reframes investment value away from base-layer tokens toward abstraction/orchestration layers; the 'OS layer' of crypto is where outsized returns sit.
insights.quote: IBM thought hardware was like where all the money was, so they didn't mind letting Microsoft own the operating system segment. Biggest blunder in the history of business... The real value is who controls abstraction.

insights.claim: Hoskinson predicts agents (not humans) will control the solver and settlement layers of the application-permission-solver-settlement stack and will route liquidity to wherever value is, breaking the liquidity dominance of Bitcoin and ETH.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Intents-based, agent-routed settlement could redistribute liquidity premia away from incumbents; underlying-chain choice becomes 'immaterial,' compressing L1 moats.
insights.quote: The solvers and settlement are not going to be controlled by humans. They'll be controlled by agents and agents are going to go where the value is.

insights.claim: Hoskinson illustrates abstracted execution by example: buying one Bitcoin could be silently sourced as 0.2 on Binance, 0.1 as wrapped BTC on Sui, plus portions on Ethereum and Solana, with the consumer seeing none of the multi-chain routing.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Multi-chain settlement makes per-venue liquidity fungible; venue/chain branding loses pricing power as agents arbitrage across all of them.
insights.quote: I want to buy a Bitcoin. Okay, well, we're going to get 0.2 of that on Binance and 0.1 of that on the Sui network as a wrapped Bitcoin and some of that on Ethereum and some of that on Solana. You as a consumer see none of that.

insights.claim: Hoskinson invokes tech-monopoly mortality (BlackBerry, Internet Explorer, AOL, MySpace, Yahoo all once had 100M+ users and died) to argue Bitcoin/ETH liquidity dominance is ephemeral and will be displaced when the technology paradigm shifts to agent-routed intents.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: A contrarian case against assuming permanent BTC/ETH dominance; technology shifts, not user loyalty, reset winners.
insights.quote: Everybody thinks they're hot and they own the entire world, but technology has a way of breaking monopolies relentlessly.

themes.theme: Bitcoin DeFi and Cardano's Pogen play

insights.claim: Hoskinson calls Bitcoin DeFi one of the biggest opportunities in the industry, sizing it at roughly a trillion dollars, and is launching Cardano's 'Pogen' as Cardano's Bitcoin DeFi product to capture it this year.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Names a specific TAM and a 2026 Cardano catalyst; Bitcoin-yield products are a sector to watch as idle BTC seeks return.
insights.quote: We have technology called Pogen. It's our Bitcoin DeFi play and it's the one of the biggest opportunities in our industry, trillion dollars of Bitcoin DeFi.

insights.claim: Hoskinson describes a concrete Bitcoin-yield flow going live on Cardano/Midnight this year: 50 BTC via Taproot bridged through BitVM to Cardano, lent against a stablecoin (USDC/USDM), deployed into a real-world asset for yield, then converted via DEX back to wrapped BTC on the Bitcoin network.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: A specific, dated product mechanic for evaluating Cardano's Bitcoin DeFi execution; BitVM/Taproot bridging and RWA yield are the components to verify on delivery.
insights.quote: I'm going to take 50 Bitcoin of my own Bitcoin... put it into Pogen... Through that bridge, BitVM, go to Cardano. And then I'm going to lend it to a stable coin... place it in a real-world asset and it's going to generate a yield.

insights.claim: Hoskinson frames the Bitcoin DeFi UX as a single 'big red button that says make me money,' where the user experiences only Bitcoin while Cardano/Midnight handle private orchestration underneath, and argues the same model scales to custodians like Xapo, BitGo and Blockchain.com holding millions of BTC.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Custodian distribution (hundreds of billions in BTC) is the scale-up path; partnerships with large BTC custodians would validate the thesis.
insights.quote: If it works there, it'll work with Zappo or BitGo or blockchain.com when you talk about millions of Bitcoin, collectively hundreds of billions of dollars of value.

insights.claim: Hoskinson predicts settlement will evolve to embed zero-knowledge proofs about the actors (jurisdiction, sanctions, collateral) so that 'settlement becomes compliance,' replacing today's value-only transactions with a built-in compliance game of 21 questions.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: ZK-native compliance could let institutions transact on-chain without doxxing counterparties; a regulatory unlock thesis for privacy-with-compliance protocols.
insights.quote: Settlement is going to start adding proofs to the layer... there's going to be some proof about the actors involved and you play a game of 21 questions and settlement becomes compliance.

insights.claim: Hoskinson notes Zcash's rising valuation is re-elevating the privacy debate and argues privacy-by-default is a 'social lubricant' and the only basis for trust, reframing privacy as enterprise/parental/IP-licensing necessity rather than a tool for bad actors.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Cites Zcash's price action as a market signal that privacy is back in favor; supports a rotation thesis into privacy-tech tokens and ZK infrastructure.
insights.quote: We see Zcash going up in terms of valuations and therefore raising the entire debate surrounding privacy.

insights.claim: Hoskinson argues the market has proven it cannot self-govern (citing FTX, Luna, meme-coin mania, hacks and scams) and that rules are necessary, but warns the US conflates legitimate compliance with geopolitical sanction agendas, eroding moral authority.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Signals support for rules-based crypto but flags US sanction overreach as a risk that drives demand for jurisdiction-neutral, selective-disclosure rails.
insights.quote: The market has proven that it cannot self-govern. It just has. You see what happened with FTX and Luna, the meme coin mania, you see the hacks and scams.

insights.claim: Hoskinson promotes 'selective disclosure' over global disclosure, arguing today's transparency has a temporal risk (information disclosed now can be weaponized decades later, e.g. Iraq's Ba'ath Party de-platforming) so users should reveal the minimum viable info per transaction.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: A durable design principle favoring minimal-disclosure credentialing; tailwind for ZK identity/credential systems over full-KYC data collection.
insights.quote: You don't want a global disclosure regime, you want a selective disclosure regime that you're able to disclose at the time of asking and you disclose the minimum viable amount of information to get the deal done.

themes.theme: Web 2.5 enterprise on-ramp and Midnight strategy

insights.claim: Hoskinson defines 'Web 2.5' as legacy Web2 companies entering crypto while keeping boards, shareholders and regulation, and observes the richest people in the industry (CZ now richer than Bill Gates, plus Tether and Circle principals) are Web 2.5 operators, not pure Web3 builders.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Directs investor attention to compliant enterprise-crypto hybrids as the wealth-creation cohort; Midnight is positioned as the open home for that segment.
insights.quote: The richest people in the industry are the Web 2.5 guys, not the Web 3 guys.

insights.claim: Hoskinson says Cardano was architected in the 2016 white paper as a settlement layer (CSL) with a network of control layers / 'partner chains,' and Midnight is one such control layer extending privacy as a modular upgrade to the whole space, not just Cardano.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Frames Midnight as cross-ecosystem infrastructure rather than a Cardano-only feature, broadening its addressable surface and fee opportunity.
insights.quote: When I wrote the white paper for Cardano back in 2016, I had this idea of Cardano CL or Cardano SL, so a settlement layer and a control layer.

insights.claim: Hoskinson reports Cardano's enterprise deals (Dish, New Balance, Ethiopia) repeatedly stalled along three axes—too complex, not safe/private enough, or compliance too hard—which motivated building Midnight as a reusable privacy+compliance network instead of bespoke one-off solutions.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Identifies the three concrete blockers (complexity, privacy, compliance) gating enterprise crypto adoption—the checklist to assess any enterprise-chain thesis.
insights.quote: They'd either stall out because it's too complicated. They'd stall out because it wasn't safe enough... Or the rules were too hard to set, i.e., the compliance side was too difficult.

insights.claim: Hoskinson frames the permissionless-vs-permissioned fight (echoing Hyperledger Fabric and R3 Corda) as recurring in the compliance era, arguing open permissionless compliance networks will beat invitation-only 'closed gardens' controlled by a few actors.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: A competitive framing pitting open compliance rails against permissioned enterprise-consortium chains; a thesis on which model wins regulated flows.
insights.quote: Are we going to have closed gardens that a handful of invitation only actors control... Or do you want to have an open permissionless system for people to go in and build their web 2.5 business?

insights.claim: Hoskinson describes 'Midnight Passport,' a phone-native account-abstraction wallet that needs no 24-word seed backup, uses the phone's secure enclave/ledger and biometrics, recovers at account creation, and via multi-chain signatures can spin up Bitcoin, Ethereum or Solana wallets with fees payable in any token.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Seedless, cross-chain phone wallets are the UX unlock; multi-chain signature wallets paying fees in any token is a feature set to benchmark competitors against.
insights.quote: You don't have to back up 24 keywords anymore... when you have multi-chain signatures, you can create a Bitcoin wallet with it or Ethereum wallet with it or Solana wallet.

insights.claim: Hoskinson lays out four immutable crypto fundamentals—money wants to be free (liquidity/velocity is king), consumers demand simplicity, stability is king (driving stablecoins), and from stability comes credit then tradable risk—arguing crypto is replaying the Baronial Age of banking but compressed from decades into years.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: A reusable lens for evaluating protocols: those that maximize liquidity velocity, simplicity and stability win; the banking-history analogy maps where the sector heads next.
insights.quote: In real time, we're kind of going through what people learned during the Baronial Age of Banking... we're reconstructing Wall Street, but instead of decades, we're doing it in years.

insights.claim: Hoskinson cites MetaMask data that ~35% of wallets are not backed up and most users (even those who shouldn't) leave funds on exchanges, as evidence that simplicity—not decentralization purity—governs real user behavior.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: Quantifies the self-custody UX failure; products that remove backup/key burden (account abstraction) address a measured pain point.
insights.quote: MetaMask, I think, 35% of their wallets don't are backed up, and most people leave their money on exchanges, including people who really really shouldn't.

insights.claim: Hoskinson dissects the Kelp DAO hack as a contagion case study: Ethereum staking lockups drove liquid-staking (Lido) and yield-looping across chains, creating hidden bridge risk; attackers turned a ~$290M problem into a multi-billion-dollar one in 42 minutes by lending stolen assets to create bad debt rather than selling, spiking Ethereum unstaking ~26,000%.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: A live example of how looped liquid-staking and bridges concentrate hidden systemic risk; flags leverage-looping and bridge exposure as the 2008-style fault lines to monitor.
insights.quote: In 42 minutes, you go from like a $290 million problem to a multi-billion dollar problem. And now there's an enormous amount of unstaking. Unstaking on Ethereum is up 26,000% or something like that.

insights.claim: Hoskinson cites NEAR intents as proof of the intents model, noting it went from nothing to billions of dollars of transactions and $71 million in protocol revenue, positioning Midnight to add privacy and take a fee on cross-chain intent settlement.
insights.speaker: Charles Hoskinson
insights.so_what: A concrete revenue proof point for intents ($71M); supports watching NEAR and other intent-settlement layers as monetizable infrastructure.
insights.quote: Near intents are a great example of that where they went from nothing to, you know, billions of dollars of transactions, $71 million in protocol revenue.

insights.claim: The interviewer discloses he holds NEAR despite not being a 'fanboy,' specifically because of the AI-agent revenue being generated on it, and asks how liquidity can spread beyond Bitcoin/ETH—an investor signal that agent-revenue is driving token selection.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Reflects real allocator behavior: rotating into agent-revenue-generating tokens regardless of brand preference; revenue from agent activity is becoming a buy thesis.
insights.quote: NEAR is also one of the protocols that I'm not really a fanboy of, but I'm actually holding that token given the fact that there's so much revenue being generated within the AI agents.

insights.claim: The episode discloses an OKX sponsorship around 'X Perps,' a fully regulated crypto-futures platform relaunched in Europe after ~1.5 years, letting users post crypto as collateral, match against OKX's global liquidity engine, and hedge positions while earning funding rates.
insights.speaker: host
insights.so_what: Sponsor read, but signals regulated crypto derivatives returning to Europe—a market-structure data point for EU-regulated perps availability.
